quiz.question: She ran a market stall selling handmade jewellery every weekend. What is a stall?
options: A small stand or booth from which goods are sold
A temporary structure set up in a public space for trading
A fixed selling point allocated by the market authority
A portable unit that could be dismantled and transported
quiz.answer: A small stand or booth from which goods are sold
quiz.explanation:
A stall is a small booth or stand — a selling point in a market.
• stall = a booth or stand for selling goods; also to delay
• From Old English: 'steall' — a place, standing
• 'Market stall', 'stall for time', 'stalled engine' — multiple senses

quiz.question: Early intervention forestalled what could have become a much larger crisis. What does 'forestall' mean?
options: To prevent by acting in advance — to anticipate and head off
To reduce the severity of a crisis through timely management
To contain a developing problem before it became unmanageable
To take action before the problem required emergency measures
quiz.answer: To prevent by acting in advance — to anticipate and head off
quiz.explanation:
To forestall means to prevent by acting first — to head off before it develops.
• forestall = to prevent by acting in advance
• From Old English: 'foresteall' — an ambush
• 'Forestall objections', 'forestall criticism' — pre-empting

quiz.question: The technician installed the new software across all workstations overnight. What does 'install' mean?
options: To set up for use — to put in place and make operational
To copy files from a source to a destination computer, as the evidence showed
To configure a program to run on a particular system
To load new applications across a network of machines
quiz.answer: To set up for use — to put in place and make operational
quiz.explanation:
To install means to set up — to put something in place and make it ready for use.
• install = to place or set up for use
• From Latin: 'installare' — to place in a stall
• 'Install software', 'install a new leader' — putting in place

quiz.question: The boat was caught in a sudden squall that came from nowhere. What is a squall?
options: A sudden violent gust of wind — often accompanied by rain
A brief but intense storm typical of certain seasons
A weather event too short to appear on a forecast
A sharp increase in wind speed that caught sailors off guard
quiz.answer: A sudden violent gust of wind — often accompanied by rain
quiz.explanation:
A squall is a sudden violent gust — a brief and fierce storm.
• squall = a sudden violent gust of wind; a baby's loud cry
• Origin uncertain; possibly Scandinavian
• 'A squall line', 'caught in a squall' — a sudden fierce wind

quiz.question: The minister stonewalled every question with the same rehearsed non-answer. What does 'stonewall' mean?
options: To obstruct or delay by refusing to cooperate or answer
To respond in a way that gave nothing away
To answer questions without providing any useful information
To use prepared answers to deflect from the substance of what was asked
quiz.answer: To obstruct or delay by refusing to cooperate or answer
quiz.explanation:
To stonewall means to obstruct — to delay by refusing to engage.
• stonewall = to delay or obstruct with evasive non-answers
• Named after General Stonewall Jackson's defensive tactics
• 'Stonewall investigators', 'stonewall questions' — blocking through non-compliance

quiz.question: In one fell swoop, the restructuring eliminated three entire departments. What does 'fell swoop' mean?
options: A single decisive action — all at once
A sudden action that resolved several problems simultaneously
A corporate decision that had far-reaching consequences
A management move that surprised everyone by its scale
quiz.answer: A single decisive action — all at once
quiz.explanation:
In one fell swoop means with a single sudden action — all at once.
• fell = fierce, cruel (archaic); swoop = a sudden plunging attack
• From Shakespeare: Macbeth
• 'In one fell swoop' = with a single sudden and complete action

quiz.question: The crowd fled pell-mell when the alarm sounded. What does 'pell-mell' mean?
options: In a disordered rush — with confused and reckless haste
As quickly as each person's own speed would allow
Without any attention to maintaining order or formation
In a way that suggested panic rather than purposeful movement
quiz.answer: In a disordered rush — with confused and reckless haste
quiz.explanation:
Pell-mell means in reckless, disordered haste — headlong and confused.
• pell-mell = in a confused, headlong rush
• From French: 'pêle-mêle' — mingled together
• 'Pell-mell flight', 'pell-mell rush' — chaotic and undisciplined speed

quiz.question: The old mill ground wheat into flour for the surrounding farms. What is a mill?
options: A building or machine for grinding grain into flour
A mechanism powered by wind or water to process grain
A facility that converts raw grain into a usable product
A traditional installation at the centre of an agricultural community
quiz.answer: A building or machine for grinding grain into flour
quiz.explanation:
A mill is a grinding facility — a building or machine for processing grain.
• mill = a building where grain is ground; a machine for grinding
• From Latin: 'mola' — a millstone
• 'Grain mill', 'pepper mill', 'mill around' — grinding or milling

quiz.question: She used a power drill to fix the shelves to the wall. What is a drill?
options: A tool that makes holes by rotating a cutting bit
An electric tool for driving screws or boring through material
A device used in construction and home improvement
A rotating machine for penetrating surfaces
quiz.answer: A tool that makes holes by rotating a cutting bit
quiz.explanation:
A drill is a boring tool — making holes by rotating a pointed cutting bit.
• drill = a tool for boring holes; a practice exercise; to bore
• From Dutch: 'drillen' — to bore
• 'Power drill', 'fire drill', 'military drill' — multiple senses

quiz.question: Distilled water has had impurities removed through boiling and condensation. What does 'distil' mean?
options: To purify by boiling and collecting the condensed vapour
To remove dissolved minerals by treating the water chemically
To filter liquid until it is free of contaminants
To process water to a level suitable for laboratory use
quiz.answer: To purify by boiling and collecting the condensed vapour
quiz.explanation:
To distil means to purify by vaporising and condensing — separating substances.
• distil = to purify by heating and condensing vapour
• From Latin: 'destillare' — to trickle down
• 'Distil spirits', 'distil wisdom' — purifying or extracting the essence

quiz.question: Parents seek to instil values in their children from the earliest age. What does 'instil' mean?
options: To gradually establish something in a person's mind
To teach values through daily example rather than explicit instruction
To introduce ideas that will shape how a child develops
To create deep beliefs through repetition and modelling
quiz.answer: To gradually establish something in a person's mind
quiz.explanation:
To instil means to introduce gradually — to establish a quality or belief incrementally.
• instil = to introduce gradually into the mind
• From Latin: 'instillare' — to pour in drop by drop
• 'Instil confidence', 'instil fear' — gradually establishing in the mind

quiz.question: Witnesses said shots had come from the direction of a grassy knoll. What is a knoll?
options: A small, rounded hill
An elevated area of ground that provided a clear line of sight
A natural feature common in park and landscape settings
A gentle rise in the ground just above the surrounding terrain
quiz.answer: A small, rounded hill
quiz.explanation:
A knoll is a small rounded hill — a slight but distinct elevation.
• knoll = a small, rounded hill or mound
• From Old English: 'cnoll' — a hilltop
• 'Grassy knoll' — associated with the Kennedy assassination; a small hill

quiz.question: The exit poll predicted the result with remarkable accuracy. What is a poll?
options: A survey or vote — a count of opinion
A prediction of an election result before all votes are counted
A sample of voters asked to share their choice after voting
A measurement of public opinion on a contested question
quiz.answer: A survey or vote — a count of opinion
quiz.explanation:
A poll is a survey or vote — a count or sampling of opinion.
• poll = a vote; a survey of opinion
• From Middle Dutch: 'pol' — head (counting heads)
• 'Exit poll', 'opinion poll', 'go to the polls' — voting or survey

quiz.question: His droll humour was perfectly dry — he never once smiled at his own jokes. What does 'droll' mean?
options: Amusing in a quiet, wry way — understated and deadpan
Clever in a way that required the audience to keep up
Subtler than the broad comedy that played to larger audiences
Appreciated most by those who shared his particular sensibility
quiz.answer: Amusing in a quiet, wry way — understated and deadpan
quiz.explanation:
Droll means quietly and wryly amusing — odd in an understated, comic way.
• droll = amusing in a quiet, strange way
• From French: 'drôle' — a rogue
• 'Droll wit', 'droll expression' — dry, understated humour

quiz.question: They strolled around the old town for an hour before finding a café. What does 'stroll' mean?
options: To walk in a leisurely and unhurried manner
To explore a new area at a pace suited to sightseeing
To move through a space without any particular destination
To walk at a speed comfortable enough for conversation
quiz.answer: To walk in a leisurely and unhurried manner
quiz.explanation:
To stroll means to walk leisurely — slowly and without urgency.
• stroll = to walk in a leisurely way
• Origin uncertain; possibly from German: 'strollen'
• 'An evening stroll', 'stroll in the park' — leisurely walking

quiz.question: Drivers had to pay a toll to use the new motorway. What is a toll?
options: A charge for using a road, bridge, or other facility
A fee collected to fund the maintenance of infrastructure
A payment required at a booth or barrier on a road
A tax levied on drivers to manage demand on congested routes
quiz.answer: A charge for using a road, bridge, or other facility
quiz.explanation:
A toll is a charge for use — a fee for using a road, bridge, or crossing.
• toll = a charge for using a road or bridge; a ringing of a bell; the cost of something
• From Latin: 'telonium' — a tax booth
• 'Toll road', 'death toll', 'toll the bell' — multiple senses

quiz.question: She had a dull ache in her lower back that had been there for weeks. What does 'dull' mean here?
options: Not sharp or intense — a low, persistent pain
Too mild to have justified seeking medical attention earlier
Consistent enough to have become part of her daily experience
Present constantly but never rising to a level she could not manage
quiz.answer: Not sharp or intense — a low, persistent pain
quiz.explanation:
Dull (of pain) means not sharp — a low, persistent, and blunted ache.
• dull = not bright; not sharp; blunted; persistent but not intense
• From Old English: 'dol' — stupid, blunted
• 'Dull ache', 'dull colour', 'dull day' — lacking sharpness or brightness

quiz.question: A gull swooped down and stole her sandwich from her hand. What is a gull?
options: A seabird common on coasts — known for scavenging
A coastal bird that nests on cliffs and rooftops
A large white seabird that follows fishing boats
A bird found throughout the British Isles and European coast
quiz.answer: A seabird common on coasts — known for scavenging
quiz.explanation:
A gull is a common seabird — widespread on coastlines.
• gull = a seabird; also: to deceive or cheat someone
• From Celtic; related to Welsh 'gwylan'
• 'Herring gull', 'gull someone' — the bird or to deceive

quiz.question: The ship's hull had to be scraped clean of barnacles before it could be repainted. What is the hull?
options: The main body of a ship — the watertight frame and shell
The part of a ship that sits below the waterline
The structural outer casing that keeps a vessel seaworthy
The lower section of a vessel that gives it buoyancy
quiz.answer: The main body of a ship — the watertight frame and shell
quiz.explanation:
The hull is the body of a ship — its structural outer frame.
• hull = the main body or frame of a ship
• From Old English: 'hulu' — a covering
• 'Hull speed', 'breach the hull' — the ship's body

quiz.question: The novel depicted a pastoral idyll of summer afternoons and golden fields. What is an idyll?
options: A scene or time of peaceful happiness — an idyllic interlude
A literary form describing the pleasures of country life
A period presented through memory as perfect and unchanging
A vision of life whose simplicity makes it universally appealing
quiz.answer: A scene or time of peaceful happiness — an idyllic interlude
quiz.explanation:
An idyll is a scene of peaceful happiness — a short peaceful interlude.
• idyll = a peaceful or picturesque scene; a poem describing such a scene
• From Greek: 'eidyllion' — a little picture
• 'A summer idyll', 'pastoral idyll' — a scene of simple, perfect peace

quiz.question: The teenager had plastered her bedroom walls with pictures of her pop idol. What is an idol?
options: A person intensely admired and worshipped — an object of devotion
A celebrity whose fame had reached a level that attracted obsession
A performer popular enough to attract a dedicated fan following
A public figure who inspired the kind of loyalty normally given to friends
quiz.answer: A person intensely admired and worshipped — an object of devotion
quiz.explanation:
An idol is an object of intense devotion — a person worshipped or adored.
• idol = an image or figure worshipped; a person intensely admired
• From Greek: 'eidolon' — an image
• 'Pop idol', 'idol worship', 'false idol' — the object of adoration

quiz.question: She sucked a menthol sweet to soothe her sore throat. What is menthol?
options: A cooling compound derived from peppermint oil
A flavouring that produces a sensation of coolness in the mouth
A natural substance used in confectionery and medicines
An ingredient that gives certain sweets and ointments their distinctive quality
quiz.answer: A cooling compound derived from peppermint oil
quiz.explanation:
Menthol is a cooling compound — derived from peppermint and used for its cooling sensation.
• menthol = a crystalline compound from peppermint giving a cooling sensation
• From Latin: 'mentha' — mint
• 'Menthol cigarette', 'menthol cream' — cooling and aromatic

quiz.question: The boat was dragged toward the whirlpool as the current strengthened. What is a whirlpool?
options: A rapidly rotating mass of water — a powerful circular current
A dangerous feature formed where strong currents converge
A spiral of water capable of drawing objects toward its centre, as the evidence showed
A naturally occurring phenomenon in rivers and tidal channels
quiz.answer: A rapidly rotating mass of water — a powerful circular current
quiz.explanation:
A whirlpool is a rotating mass of water — a powerful circular current.
• whirlpool = a rotating body of water
• From 'whirl' + 'pool'
• 'Caught in a whirlpool', 'whirlpool of activity' — circular, powerful motion

quiz.question: She selected a spool of thread in a matching colour for the repair. What is a spool?
options: A cylinder on which thread or film is wound
A small reel used to store and dispense sewing thread
A rotating holder that feeds thread to a sewing machine
A storage device for thread that prevents it from tangling
quiz.answer: A cylinder on which thread or film is wound
quiz.explanation:
A spool is a cylinder for winding — thread, wire, or film is wound around it.
• spool = a cylinder on which thread, wire, or film is wound
• From Middle Dutch: 'spoele'
• 'Thread spool', 'film spool' — a reel for winding material

quiz.question: They went from house to house singing carols on Christmas Eve. What is a carol?
options: A song associated with a religious festival — especially Christmas
A traditional hymn sung in the days leading up to Christmas, as the evidence showed
A piece of music performed communally at festive times
A song performed door-to-door as a seasonal tradition
quiz.answer: A song associated with a religious festival — especially Christmas
quiz.explanation:
A carol is a festive song — especially one sung at Christmas.
• carol = a festive song, especially at Christmas
• From Old French: 'carole' — a ring dance
• 'Christmas carol', 'go carolling' — festive communal singing

quiz.question: A pistol is a firearm small enough to be fired with one hand. What is a pistol?
options: A small handgun designed to be fired with one hand
A compact firearm favoured by soldiers and police
A weapon whose size made it easier to carry concealed
A hand-held firearm that was the standard sidearm
quiz.answer: A small handgun designed to be fired with one hand
quiz.explanation:
A pistol is a small handgun — designed for one-handed operation.
• pistol = a small firearm operated with one hand
• From Czech: 'píšt'al' — a pipe
• 'Pistol shot', 'start pistol', 'pistol whip' — small firearm

quiz.question: The brochure extolled the virtues of country living. What does 'extol' mean?
options: To praise enthusiastically — to speak highly of
To describe in language designed to attract potential buyers
To present the advantages of something in the most positive terms
To market a product by emphasising its best qualities
quiz.answer: To praise enthusiastically — to speak highly of
quiz.explanation:
To extol means to praise highly — to speak of with enthusiastic approval.
• extol = to praise enthusiastically
• From Latin: 'extollere' — to raise
• 'Extol the benefits', 'extol someone's achievements' — high praise

quiz.question: The local landowner was a surly churl who drove away anyone who strayed onto his land. What is a churl?
options: A rude, bad-tempered person — an ill-mannered lout
A person whose wealth gave him no obligation of courtesy
A landowner who resented any intrusion on his privacy
A person whose manner was calculated to discourage visitors
quiz.answer: A rude, bad-tempered person — an ill-mannered lout
quiz.explanation:
A churl is a rude, boorish person — surly and ill-mannered.
• churl = a rude, bad-tempered person; historically a peasant
• From Old English: 'ceorl' — a man, peasant
• 'A complete churl', 'churlish behavior' — rudeness and bad temper

quiz.question: The mechanics gave the engine a complete overhaul before the race. What is an overhaul?
options: A thorough examination and repair — a complete renovation
A service procedure that exceeded the standard maintenance schedule
A rebuilding of major components to restore original performance
A comprehensive check that identified and resolved all problems
quiz.answer: A thorough examination and repair — a complete renovation
quiz.explanation:
An overhaul is a thorough inspection and repair — completely going over something.
• overhaul = a thorough examination and repair
• From 'over' + 'haul' — hauling over entirely
• 'Engine overhaul', 'overhaul the system' — thorough repair or reform

quiz.question: The bear mauled the hiker before being driven away. What does 'maul' mean?
options: To attack and injure with tearing or crushing force
To cause injuries severe enough to require hospitalisation
To inflict wounds through repeated clawing or biting
To overcome through superior size and strength
quiz.answer: To attack and injure with tearing or crushing force
quiz.explanation:
To maul means to attack and injure brutally — to tear and crush.
• maul = to attack and injure by tearing; to handle roughly
• From Old French: 'mail' — a hammer
• 'Mauled by a bear', 'mauled by critics' — rough, damaging treatment

quiz.question: The resourceful leader found solutions even when the obvious routes were blocked. What does 'resourceful' mean?
options: Able to find quick and clever ways around difficulties
Experienced enough to recognise solutions others had overlooked
Creative under pressure in a way that inspired those around her
Skilled at identifying what options remained when the obvious had failed
quiz.answer: Able to find quick and clever ways around difficulties
quiz.explanation:
Resourceful means finding ways around problems — inventive and capable under pressure.
• resourceful = able to find ways of dealing with problems
• From 'resource' + '-ful'
• 'Resourceful manager', 'resourceful solution' — inventive problem-solving

quiz.question: The vengeful spirit of the betrayed queen haunted the castle in the old legend. What does 'vengeful' mean?
options: Seeking revenge — driven by the desire for retribution
Unable to rest until justice had been done for the wrong suffered
Motivated by anger at what had been done rather than by reason
Associated in folklore with those who had been wronged in death
quiz.answer: Seeking revenge — driven by the desire for retribution
quiz.explanation:
Vengeful means seeking revenge — motivated by the desire for retaliation.
• vengeful = wanting to harm someone as punishment for harm done
• From 'vengeance' + '-ful'
• 'Vengeful act', 'vengeful god' — motivated by the desire for revenge

quiz.question: He was a dutiful son who visited his parents every Sunday without fail. What does 'dutiful' mean?
options: Conscientiously fulfilling one's duties — obedient and responsible
Loyal in a way that required sustained effort over many years
Consistent in a way that earned the admiration of those who knew him
Reliable in meeting obligations that he had set for himself
quiz.answer: Conscientiously fulfilling one's duties — obedient and responsible
quiz.explanation:
Dutiful means conscientiously doing one's duty — reliably fulfilling obligations.
• dutiful = conscientiously fulfilling one's duty
• From 'duty' + '-ful'
• 'Dutiful employee', 'dutiful child' — reliably meeting obligations

quiz.question: The court found the company guilty of wilful neglect of its safety obligations. What does 'wilful' mean?
options: Deliberate and intentional — done on purpose
Serious enough to constitute a criminal rather than a civil matter
So persistent that it could not be explained as an oversight
Committed with knowledge that harm was likely to result
quiz.answer: Deliberate and intentional — done on purpose
quiz.explanation:
Wilful means deliberate — done intentionally rather than accidentally.
• wilful = done deliberately; not accidental; obstinate
• From 'will' + '-ful'
• 'Wilful misconduct', 'wilful child' — deliberate and intentional

quiz.question: She returned home to blissful silence after the noise of the city. What does 'blissful' mean?
options: Full of perfect happiness — completely contented
Peaceful in a way that provided exactly what was needed
Preferable in every way to the environment she had just left
So quiet that the contrast with the city felt like a physical relief
quiz.answer: Full of perfect happiness — completely contented
quiz.explanation:
Blissful means perfectly happy — full of complete and undisturbed contentment.
• blissful = full of happiness
• From Old English: 'bliss' — perfect happiness
• 'Blissful ignorance', 'blissful silence' — perfectly happy, undisturbed

quiz.question: She had a fitful sleep, waking repeatedly throughout the night. What does 'fitful' mean?
options: Intermittent and irregular — marked by stops and starts
Light enough to be disrupted by any sound in the house, as the evidence showed
Insufficient to provide the rest her body required
Disrupted by anxiety rather than by any external cause
quiz.answer: Intermittent and irregular — marked by stops and starts
quiz.explanation:
Fitful means irregular and intermittent — occurring in fits and starts.
• fitful = occurring in irregular bursts; not sustained
• From 'fit' — a sudden attack or outburst
• 'Fitful sleep', 'fitful progress' — irregular, starting and stopping

quiz.question: The business mogul had built an empire worth billions from a single shop. What is a mogul?
options: A very powerful and wealthy business person
An entrepreneur whose success had become a matter of public interest
A self-made billionaire who had become influential beyond business
A person who controlled a large part of a particular industry
quiz.answer: A very powerful and wealthy business person
quiz.explanation:
A mogul is a powerful, wealthy figure — especially in business or media.
• mogul = a very powerful and wealthy person
• From 'Mughal' — the Mughal emperors of India
• 'Media mogul', 'Hollywood mogul' — a dominant, powerful figure

quiz.question: The court agreed to annul the marriage on the grounds that it was never legally valid. What does 'annul' mean?
options: To declare legally void — to cancel as if it never existed
To end a marriage that had broken down irretrievably
To reverse a legal arrangement through a court order
To formally acknowledge that the legal requirements were not met
quiz.answer: To declare legally void — to cancel as if it never existed
quiz.explanation:
To annul means to declare void — to cancel completely as if it never occurred.
• annul = to declare legally void; to cancel
• From Latin: 'annullare' — to reduce to nothing
• 'Annul a marriage', 'annul a contract' — making it legally void

quiz.question: A foul smell from the drains greeted them as they opened the door. What does 'foul' mean?
options: Extremely unpleasant — offensive to the senses
Strong enough to be detectable from the street
Indicating a problem that had been present for some time
Too unpleasant to be ignored or attributed to a minor cause
quiz.answer: Extremely unpleasant — offensive to the senses
quiz.explanation:
Foul means extremely offensive — unpleasant and disgusting.
• foul = very unpleasant; offensive; against the rules
• From Old English: 'ful' — rotten
• 'Foul smell', 'foul play', 'foul weather' — offensive, against rules, or bad

quiz.question: She contacted the Italian consul to obtain the necessary documentation. What is a consul?
options: An official appointed by a government to represent its interests abroad
A diplomat responsible for assisting citizens in a foreign country
A government representative who issues visas and travel documents
An official who acts for their government in commercial and administrative matters
quiz.answer: An official appointed by a government to represent its interests abroad
quiz.explanation:
A consul is an official representing a country's interests abroad — especially commercial and civic.
• consul = an official representing government interests abroad
• From Latin: 'consul' — a Roman magistrate
• 'Consulate', 'consul general' — diplomatic representative

quiz.question: He used an awl to punch holes through the leather before stitching. What is an awl?
options: A pointed tool for making holes in leather or wood
A sharp implement used in leatherwork and bookbinding
A hand tool that pierces material to allow thread to pass through
A traditional craft tool used before mechanical punches were available
quiz.answer: A pointed tool for making holes in leather or wood
quiz.explanation:
An awl is a sharp-pointed tool — used for piercing holes.
• awl = a pointed tool for making holes in leather or wood
• From Old English: 'æl'
• 'Cobbler's awl', 'sewing awl' — a hole-piercing tool

quiz.question: Researchers trawled through archives looking for relevant evidence. What does 'trawl' mean?
options: To search thoroughly — to drag through to find what is needed
To spend large amounts of time reading unpublished material, as the evidence showed
To access records that required authorisation to consult
To review a large body of material systematically
quiz.answer: To search thoroughly — to drag through to find what is needed
quiz.explanation:
To trawl means to search thoroughly — to drag through a large body of material.
• trawl = to fish by dragging a net; to search thoroughly
• From Dutch: 'tragelen' — to drag
• 'Trawl the internet', 'trawl for candidates' — systematic searching

quiz.question: The oracle consulted in the ancient world was sometimes called a sibyl. What is a sibyl?
options: A woman in antiquity believed to have prophetic powers
A priestess who delivered prophecies at a sacred site
A figure consulted before major decisions in the ancient world
An oracle associated with a particular shrine or sanctuary
quiz.answer: A woman in antiquity believed to have prophetic powers
quiz.explanation:
A sibyl is a prophetess — a woman of antiquity believed to utter divine prophecies.
• sibyl = a woman believed to have prophetic powers
• From Greek: 'sibylla' — prophetess
• 'Sibylline oracle', 'the Cumaean Sibyl' — ancient female prophet

quiz.question: A laser beam was used to cut the metal with extraordinary precision. What is a beam?
options: A ray of light or radiation — a directed flow of energy
The focused output of a laser device at its operating intensity
A narrow line of light that could be directed at a target
The electromagnetic output used in precision cutting and surgery
quiz.answer: A ray of light or radiation — a directed flow of energy
quiz.explanation:
A beam is a ray — a directed flow of light or radiation.
• beam = a ray of light; a structural horizontal bar; to smile broadly
• From Old English: 'beam' — a tree, a ray
• 'Laser beam', 'beam of sunlight', 'beam with pride' — multiple senses

quiz.question: She left a generous seam allowance when cutting the fabric. What is a seam?
options: A line where two pieces of fabric are stitched together
The join between two sections of cloth in a garment
The sewn edge that holds two pieces of material in place
The boundary between two sections that is reinforced by stitching
quiz.answer: A line where two pieces of fabric are stitched together
quiz.explanation:
A seam is a line of stitching joining fabrics — or any similar junction.
• seam = a line of stitching joining two pieces of material
• From Old English: 'seam' — a stitch
• 'Seam allowance', 'seam in rock', 'bursting at the seams' — a joint line

quiz.question: The dental filling was made from an amalgam of metals. What is an amalgam?
options: A mixture of different elements combined into one
A combination of metals used in specialist applications
A blend of disparate components that formed a usable material
A compound produced by combining mercury with other metals
quiz.answer: A mixture of different elements combined into one
quiz.explanation:
An amalgam is a mixture — a combination of different elements.
• amalgam = a mixture or blend; specifically mercury mixed with another metal
• From Medieval Latin: 'amalgama'
• 'Dental amalgam', 'an amalgam of styles' — a blend of different things

quiz.question: The accident caused a logjam that stretched back five miles. What is a logjam?
options: A deadlock or blockage — an impasse preventing movement or progress
A congestion caused by an event that blocked the normal flow, as the evidence showed
A traffic situation requiring emergency services to clear
A backup severe enough to affect roads several miles away
quiz.answer: A deadlock or blockage — an impasse preventing movement or progress
quiz.explanation:
A logjam is a blockage — originally logs jammed in a river, now any impasse.
• logjam = a deadlock; a blockage preventing progress
• From log drives on rivers where logs jammed and stopped movement
• 'Traffic logjam', 'break the logjam' — a blockage or deadlock

quiz.question: She ordered a bowl of clam chowder, made from freshly caught clams. What is a clam?
options: A bivalve mollusc with two shells — used as food
A shellfish found in sand and mud in coastal areas
A marine creature harvested commercially for consumption
A seafood ingredient common in American coastal cooking
quiz.answer: A bivalve mollusc with two shells — used as food
quiz.explanation:
A clam is a bivalve mollusc — a shellfish with two hinged shells.
• clam = a bivalve mollusc; to clam up = to refuse to speak
• From Old English: 'clamm' — a bond or grip
• 'Clam chowder', 'clam up' — the shellfish or to stay silent

quiz.question: The valley's fertile loam supported rich crops for centuries. What is loam?
options: A rich, fertile soil — a mixture of sand, clay, and organic matter
The type of soil considered ideal for most agricultural purposes
A dark, heavy earth that retained moisture without becoming waterlogged
Soil whose composition produced the highest agricultural yields
quiz.answer: A rich, fertile soil — a mixture of sand, clay, and organic matter
quiz.explanation:
Loam is fertile soil — a balanced mixture ideal for agriculture.
• loam = a rich soil of sand, clay, and organic matter
• From Old English: 'lam' — clay
• 'Loamy soil', 'fertile loam' — the best agricultural soil

quiz.question: The government rammed the legislation through parliament before the recess. What does 'ram through' mean?
options: To force something through with speed and without proper scrutiny
To use a parliamentary majority to pass legislation quickly
To schedule a vote before opponents could organise their resistance
To bypass the normal committee process to achieve a quick result
quiz.answer: To force something through with speed and without proper scrutiny
quiz.explanation:
To ram through means to force past obstacles — to push through quickly and forcefully.
• ram = to drive or force with impact
• From 'ram' — a male sheep known for butting
• 'Ram through legislation' = force it through against resistance

quiz.question: The bar chart showed sales figures for each month of the year. What is a diagram?
options: A visual representation that clarifies information — a chart or drawing
A graphical display designed to make data easy to interpret
A presentation tool that replaced text with visual information
A structured visual that allowed patterns in data to be identified, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A visual representation that clarifies information — a chart or drawing
quiz.explanation:
A diagram is a visual representation — a drawing that clarifies information.
• diagram = a simplified drawing showing information visually
• From Greek: 'diagramma' — a figure
• 'Flow diagram', 'bar chart', 'Venn diagram' — visual representations

quiz.question: The word 'listen' is an anagram of 'silent'. What is an anagram?
options: A word formed by rearranging the letters of another word
A word that contains the same letters in a different order
A linguistic puzzle in which one word is hidden inside another
A word whose letters can be rearranged to spell something else
quiz.answer: A word formed by rearranging the letters of another word
quiz.explanation:
An anagram is a rearrangement — a word or phrase formed from another's letters.
• anagram = a word formed by rearranging the letters of another
• From Greek: 'ana-' (again) + 'gramma' (letter)
• 'Listen' / 'silent', 'eat' / 'ate' — anagrams

quiz.question: She was known for her witty epigrams that summed up complex situations in a single line. What is an epigram?
options: A brief, witty, and pointed saying or poem
A memorable phrase that expressed a truth in compressed form
A remark so well-constructed it was worth quoting
A short statement that combined wisdom with humour
quiz.answer: A brief, witty, and pointed saying or poem
quiz.explanation:
An epigram is a short, witty saying — a brief and pointed expression.
• epigram = a brief, pointed saying; a short witty poem
• From Greek: 'epigramma' — an inscription
• 'Oscar Wilde's epigrams', 'witty epigram' — brief, pointed wit

quiz.question: She redeemed her supermarket vouchers at the checkout. What does 'redeem' mean?
options: To exchange for a benefit — to convert into value
To present a voucher and receive the discount it promised
To use an entitlement that had been accumulated over time
To collect the value attached to a token or certificate
quiz.answer: To exchange for a benefit — to convert into value
quiz.explanation:
To redeem means to exchange for value — to convert a token into its benefit.
• redeem = to exchange; to compensate for faults; to save from sin
• From Latin: 'redimere' — to buy back
• 'Redeem a voucher', 'redeem oneself' — exchanging or making good

quiz.question: The general devised a cunning stratagem that deceived the enemy completely. What is a stratagem?
options: A clever scheme or trick — especially to gain an advantage
A military plan designed to mislead an opponent
A tactic that relied on deception rather than strength, as the evidence showed
A ruse that exploited the enemy's false assumptions
quiz.answer: A clever scheme or trick — especially to gain an advantage
quiz.explanation:
A stratagem is a cunning plan — a scheme devised to gain an advantage.
• stratagem = a plan or scheme to gain an advantage
• From Greek: 'strategema' — a piece of generalship
• 'Cunning stratagem', 'military stratagem' — a clever trick or ruse

quiz.question: She hemmed the skirt to the correct length before the event. What does 'hem' mean?
options: To fold and stitch the edge of a fabric
To alter the length of a garment by sewing the lower edge
To finish a piece of cloth so its edge does not fray
To take in a garment to the desired measurement
quiz.answer: To fold and stitch the edge of a fabric
quiz.explanation:
To hem means to stitch the edge of fabric — creating a neat, finished border.
• hem = the edge of a piece of cloth folded and stitched; to stitch an edge
• From Old English: 'hem' — a border
• 'Hem a skirt', 'hem in' — a stitched edge or to surround

quiz.question: The players stood for the national anthem before the match began. What is an anthem?
options: A song of praise or loyalty — especially a national one
A piece of music that identifies a nation in an official context
A patriotic song performed at state occasions and sporting events
A formal piece of music used to open official ceremonies
quiz.answer: A song of praise or loyalty — especially a national one
quiz.explanation:
An anthem is a song of praise or national identity — a formal hymn or song.
• anthem = a rousing or official song; a hymn of praise
• From Old English: 'antefn' — antiphon
• 'National anthem', 'sporting anthem' — a song of belonging

quiz.question: The power cut plunged the city into mayhem. What is mayhem?
options: Violent disorder and chaos — extreme confusion and damage
A situation too dangerous to enter without emergency services
The kind of disruption that required military assistance to control
A breakdown of order across multiple areas of a city simultaneously
quiz.answer: Violent disorder and chaos — extreme confusion and damage
quiz.explanation:
Mayhem is violent chaos — extreme disorder and damage.
• mayhem = violent disorder; chaos
• From Old French: 'mahem' — injury
• 'Create mayhem', 'absolute mayhem' — chaotic, violent disorder

quiz.question: The elder's apothegms were quoted throughout the community for their practical wisdom. What is an apothegm?
options: A terse, instructive saying — a pithy maxim
A proverb that had been passed down across generations
A statement that compressed a lifetime of experience into a phrase
A saying so memorable it was quoted rather than paraphrased
quiz.answer: A terse, instructive saying — a pithy maxim
quiz.explanation:
An apothegm is a pithy saying — a terse and instructive maxim.
• apothegm = a short, instructive saying
• From Greek: 'apophthegma' — something plainly spoken
• Similar to epigram but more instructive; 'wise apothegm' = practical wisdom

quiz.question: The new research challenged the dominant teaching paradigm in language education. What is a paradigm?
options: A typical pattern or model — the prevailing framework for thinking
The body of theory that guided practitioners in a field
A set of assumptions so widely shared they were rarely questioned
The established approach that determined how a discipline operated, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: A typical pattern or model — the prevailing framework for thinking
quiz.explanation:
A paradigm is a model or framework — the prevailing pattern for understanding.
• paradigm = a typical example; the prevailing theoretical framework
• From Greek: 'paradeigma' — a pattern
• 'Paradigm shift', 'teaching paradigm' — the dominant model

quiz.question: The film received critical acclaim despite performing poorly at the box office. What is acclaim?
options: Enthusiastic praise and public approval
Recognition from professional reviewers as a work of quality
A positive critical reception that contrasted with commercial results
Praise strong enough to generate awards nominations
quiz.answer: Enthusiastic praise and public approval
quiz.explanation:
Acclaim is enthusiastic approval — public praise or recognition.
• acclaim = enthusiastic public praise; to praise enthusiastically
• From Latin: 'acclamare' — to cry out to
• 'Critical acclaim', 'universal acclaim' — enthusiastic public approval

quiz.question: The actor declaimed the monologue with passion and precision. What does 'declaim' mean?
options: To recite or speak aloud with force and expression
To deliver a text with the dramatic intensity it demanded
To perform verse with attention to rhythm and emphasis
To project a text to an audience using controlled voice technique
quiz.answer: To recite or speak aloud with force and expression
quiz.explanation:
To declaim means to recite with rhetorical force — to speak aloud dramatically.
• declaim = to recite aloud with force and feeling
• From Latin: 'declamare' — to practice speaking
• 'Declaim poetry', 'declaim against injustice' — passionate oral delivery

quiz.question: The Netherlands has reclaimed vast areas of land from the sea. What does 'reclaim' mean?
options: To recover something previously lost or given up
To take back land that was covered by water
To use engineering to create usable ground from the sea
To reverse the natural process by which the sea covered the land
quiz.answer: To recover something previously lost or given up
quiz.explanation:
To reclaim means to recover — to take back something previously surrendered or lost.
• reclaim = to retrieve; to recover waste material; to take back land
• From Latin: 're-' + 'clamare' (to call)
• 'Reclaim land', 'reclaim a prize', 'reclaim materials' — recovering

quiz.question: The company disclaimed all responsibility for the accident. What does 'disclaim' mean?
options: To deny having responsibility — to formally repudiate
To issue a public statement denying any obligation
To assert through legal channels that liability did not apply
To reject a claim before it could become the subject of litigation
quiz.answer: To deny having responsibility — to formally repudiate
quiz.explanation:
To disclaim means to formally deny — to repudiate responsibility or rights.
• disclaim = to deny; to formally renounce a claim
• From Old French: 'desclamer' — to deny
• 'Disclaim liability', 'disclaimer' — denying responsibility

quiz.question: She acted on an idle whim and bought a one-way ticket to Paris. What is a whim?
options: A sudden and passing fancy — a capricious impulse
A decision made without a clear plan or reason
An impulse she would not have been able to explain rationally
A spontaneous choice that surprised even herself
quiz.answer: A sudden and passing fancy — a capricious impulse
quiz.explanation:
A whim is a sudden fancy — an impulsive, capricious desire.
• whim = a sudden wish or desire; a capricious notion
• Origin uncertain
• 'On a whim', 'idle whim', 'whimsical' — a passing, unexplained fancy

quiz.question: She wore a classic denim jacket over her dress. What is denim?
options: A sturdy cotton fabric — originally used for workwear
A woven material that became associated with casual wear
A fabric associated with jeans and other casual garments
A hard-wearing textile produced in a twill weave
quiz.answer: A sturdy cotton fabric — originally used for workwear
quiz.explanation:
Denim is a sturdy cotton twill fabric — originally used for durable work clothing.
• denim = a sturdy cotton twill fabric
• From French: 'de Nîmes' — from Nîmes (the French city where it was made)
• 'Denim jeans', 'denim jacket' — the iconic workwear fabric

quiz.question: An interim manager was appointed to lead the company while the search continued. What does 'interim' mean?
options: Temporary — in the meantime, until a permanent solution is found
Appointed without the full authority of a permanent holder
Serving for a defined period before a replacement was confirmed, as the evidence showed
Filling a role that had become vacant unexpectedly
quiz.answer: Temporary — in the meantime, until a permanent solution is found
quiz.explanation:
Interim means temporary — serving in the meantime.
• interim = in the meantime; temporary
• From Latin: 'interim' — meanwhile
• 'Interim manager', 'interim report', 'in the interim' — temporary

quiz.question: Pilgrims had travelled from every corner of the world to visit the sacred site. What is a pilgrim?
options: A person who travels to a sacred place as an act of devotion
A believer who makes a journey to fulfil a religious obligation
A traveller whose journey is itself understood as a spiritual act
Someone who visits a holy site as part of a recognised tradition
quiz.answer: A person who travels to a sacred place as an act of devotion
quiz.explanation:
A pilgrim is a religious traveller — one who journeys to a sacred site.
• pilgrim = a person who travels to a holy place
• From Latin: 'peregrinus' — a foreigner, wanderer
• 'Pilgrim's progress', 'pilgrimage' — the journey to a sacred place

quiz.question: Her prim manners made some guests feel they were being judged. What does 'prim' mean?
options: Stiffly correct and formal — disapproving of informality
Formal in a way that could make relaxed conversation difficult
Careful about propriety to a degree that made others self-conscious
Too aware of social rules to be able to ignore when others broke them
quiz.answer: Stiffly correct and formal — disapproving of informality
quiz.explanation:
Prim means excessively formal and proper — disapproving of anything relaxed.
• prim = stiffly correct; disapproving of informality
• Probably from 'prime' — excellent
• 'Prim manner', 'prim and proper' — overly correct and formal

quiz.question: She quoted the regulation verbatim to ensure there was no misunderstanding. What does 'verbatim' mean?
options: Word for word — in exactly the same words
Precisely enough to carry legal force in the context
Accurately enough to prevent any misinterpretation
In the exact form in which the original text was written
quiz.answer: Word for word — in exactly the same words
quiz.explanation:
Verbatim means word for word — in exactly the same words as the original.
• verbatim = in exactly the same words; word for word
• From Latin: 'verbum' — a word
• 'Quote verbatim', 'verbatim transcript' — exact reproduction

quiz.question: She tackled the project with vim and vigour that surprised her colleagues. What is vim?
options: Lively energy and enthusiasm
A determination to succeed that was visible from the start
An attitude to work that could not be explained by the circumstances
The kind of energy that could not be sustained indefinitely
quiz.answer: Lively energy and enthusiasm
quiz.explanation:
Vim means vigorous energy — lively, spirited enthusiasm.
• vim = lively energy; vigour
• From Latin: 'vis' — energy, force
• 'Vim and vigour', 'full of vim' — energetic enthusiasm

quiz.question: He lived by the maxim that one should treat others as one wishes to be treated. What is a maxim?
options: A short, general statement of a rule or principle
A principle so widely accepted it required no further justification
A guiding rule expressed in memorable and portable form
A statement of principle that one applies consistently
quiz.answer: A short, general statement of a rule or principle
quiz.explanation:
A maxim is a general principle — a short, pithy statement of a rule.
• maxim = a short statement of a general principle
• From Latin: 'maxima' — greatest proposition
• 'Live by a maxim', 'maxim of behaviour' — a general rule

quiz.question: Her words were a balm to the distressed family. What is a balm?
options: Something that soothes or heals — a comfort
A response so well-judged it reduced the family's distress
The kind of comfort that made the situation feel manageable
Words chosen to reduce anxiety rather than to provide information
quiz.answer: Something that soothes or heals — a comfort
quiz.explanation:
A balm is a soothing agent — something that relieves pain or distress.
• balm = a healing ointment; something that soothes
• From Latin: 'balsamum' — balsam
• 'A balm to the soul', 'balm for grief' — a comfort or soothing agent

quiz.question: His work occupied the realm of art rather than commerce. What is a realm?
options: A domain or sphere — a field of activity or thought
The area of human experience most relevant to his work
A category of human endeavour defined by its values and purposes
A sphere of activity governed by its own standards and criteria
quiz.answer: A domain or sphere — a field of activity or thought
quiz.explanation:
A realm is a domain — a sphere of activity, authority, or knowledge.
• realm = a domain; a kingdom
• From Old French: 'reaume' — a kingdom
• 'The realm of science', 'realm of imagination' — domain or sphere

quiz.question: The congregation sang a psalm at the opening of the service. What is a psalm?
options: A sacred song or hymn — especially from the Hebrew scriptures
A piece of music performed as part of a religious observance
A religious poem set to music and used in worship
One of the hymns collected in the Old Testament book of Psalms
quiz.answer: A sacred song or hymn — especially from the Hebrew scriptures
quiz.explanation:
A psalm is a sacred song — especially one from the Book of Psalms.
• psalm = a sacred song or hymn
• From Greek: 'psalmos' — a song sung to a harp
• 'Sing a psalm', 'the Psalms of David' — sacred hymns

quiz.question: She felt a sudden qualm of conscience as she signed the document. What is a qualm?
options: A feeling of doubt or unease — a pang of conscience
A sense that she had not considered all the consequences
A doubt that arrived at the moment it was least convenient
An instinctive resistance to an action she was about to take
quiz.answer: A feeling of doubt or unease — a pang of conscience
quiz.explanation:
A qualm is a sudden doubt or scruple — a pang of conscience or unease.
• qualm = a feeling of doubt, unease, or scruple
• From Old English: 'cwealm' — pain, death
• 'Without qualms', 'a qualm of conscience' — a moment of doubt

quiz.question: The old elm that had stood in the churchyard for two hundred years was felled by disease. What is an elm?
options: A large deciduous tree — once common in the English countryside
A woodland tree that was devastated by Dutch elm disease
A species of tree associated with hedgerows and village greens, as the evidence showed
A tall, arching tree that once defined the English landscape
quiz.answer: A large deciduous tree — once common in the English countryside
quiz.explanation:
An elm is a large deciduous tree — Ulmus species, once iconic in Britain.
• elm = a large tree of the genus Ulmus
• From Old English: 'elm'
• 'Elm Avenue', 'Dutch elm disease' — the tree once common in English landscapes

quiz.question: When the CEO fell ill, her deputy stepped in to take the helm. What does 'take the helm' mean?
options: To assume control or leadership
To manage the day-to-day operations of the business
To make decisions that only the CEO was normally authorised to take
To represent the organisation in public while the CEO recovered
quiz.answer: To assume control or leadership
quiz.explanation:
To take the helm means to assume leadership — to take control.
• helm = the wheel or tiller that steers a ship; figuratively, leadership
• From Old English: 'helma' — a rudder
• 'Take the helm', 'at the helm' — in charge, steering

quiz.question: She was so whelmed by grief she could hardly speak. What does 'whelm' mean?
options: To submerge or overcome completely — to engulf
To experience a loss so significant it left her unable to function
To be affected by an emotion too powerful to conceal
To feel sorrow at a level that required time before it could be expressed
quiz.answer: To submerge or overcome completely — to engulf
quiz.explanation:
To whelm means to submerge or overwhelm — to engulf completely.
• whelm = to engulf; to overwhelm
• From Old English: 'hwelfan' — to cover over
• Now rare alone; 'overwhelm' is the common form

quiz.question: The scale and grandeur of the canyon overwhelmed the senses of every visitor. What does 'overwhelm' mean?
options: To overpower completely — to submerge under an irresistible force
To produce a physical and emotional response that words could not capture
To exceed the capacity of normal experience to prepare for
To affect those present in a way that made speech temporarily impossible
quiz.answer: To overpower completely — to submerge under an irresistible force
quiz.explanation:
To overwhelm means to overpower entirely — to submerge under an unstoppable force.
• overwhelm = to overpower; to defeat completely
• From 'over-' + 'whelm'
• 'Overwhelmed with work', 'overwhelmed with emotion' — completely overpowered

quiz.question: He had a low boredom threshold — he needed constant stimulation. What is a threshold?
options: The level at which something begins to have an effect
The point at which boredom became impossible to conceal
The boundary between tolerating a situation and being unable to
The measure of how much of something a person could endure
quiz.answer: The level at which something begins to have an effect
quiz.explanation:
A threshold is the level at which something begins — the starting point of an effect.
• threshold = the point at which something begins; the minimum required
• From Old English: 'therscold' — the floor of a doorway
• 'Pain threshold', 'boredom threshold', 'cross the threshold' — the critical level

quiz.question: Peasants living under serfdom were bound to the land and owed labour to their lord. What is serfdom?
options: A system in which labourers are legally bound to a landowner
A feudal arrangement that provided security at the cost of freedom
A social system in which most of the population had no legal rights
The agricultural labour system of medieval European society
quiz.answer: A system in which labourers are legally bound to a landowner
quiz.explanation:
Serfdom is a system of unfree labour — serfs were bound to the land and lord.
• serfdom = the condition of a serf; bondage to land and lord
• From Latin: 'servus' — a slave
• 'End of serfdom', 'feudal serfdom' — bound, unfree agricultural labour

quiz.question: The survey used a random sample of 1,000 adults to estimate national opinion. What is a sample?
options: A small part selected to represent the whole
A group chosen to stand in for a much larger population
A subset large enough to produce statistically valid results
A portion extracted from a population for study
quiz.answer: A small part selected to represent the whole
quiz.explanation:
A sample is a representative portion — a small part chosen to stand for the whole.
• sample = a small part taken to represent the whole
• From Old French: 'essample' — an example
• 'Random sample', 'sample size', 'sample the food' — a representative part

quiz.question: That all humans have equal worth is treated as a self-evident axiom in democratic thought. What is an axiom?
options: A statement accepted as obviously true — a self-evident principle
A foundational claim on which other arguments are built
A principle so widely accepted it requires no further proof
A philosophical starting point that structures subsequent reasoning
quiz.answer: A statement accepted as obviously true — a self-evident principle
quiz.explanation:
An axiom is a self-evident principle — a statement accepted without proof.
• axiom = a statement accepted as obviously true
• From Greek: 'axioma' — what is deemed worthy
• 'Mathematical axiom', 'self-evident axiom' — a foundational truth

quiz.question: The post-war economic boom transformed living standards across the developed world. What is a boom?
options: A period of rapid growth and prosperity
A phase of expansion that benefited most sectors of the economy
A sustained increase in output that raised incomes for many
A period of economic conditions significantly better than normal
quiz.answer: A period of rapid growth and prosperity
quiz.explanation:
A boom is a period of rapid growth — an economic surge.
• boom = a period of sudden increase and prosperity; a loud deep sound
• From 'boom' — the sound of expansion
• 'Economic boom', 'baby boom', 'boom and bust' — a surge of growth

quiz.question: The clock had been a family heirloom passed down for six generations. What is an heirloom?
options: A valued object passed down through generations of a family
A possession that carried the history of those who had owned it
An object whose value was as much sentimental as monetary
Something inherited that could not be replaced if lost
quiz.answer: A valued object passed down through generations of a family
quiz.explanation:
An heirloom is a precious inherited object — passed down through a family.
• heirloom = a valuable object that has been in a family for generations
• From 'heir' + 'loom' (a tool)
• 'Family heirloom', 'precious heirloom' — inherited and cherished

quiz.question: The groom waited at the altar as the bride made her entrance. What is a groom?
options: A man about to be or who has just been married
The partner who waits at the altar during a wedding ceremony
The male participant in a wedding who stands beside the bride
A man on his wedding day before the ceremony is complete
quiz.answer: A man about to be or who has just been married
quiz.explanation:
A groom is a bridegroom — a man on his wedding day.
• groom = a bridegroom; also to tend and care for animals
• From Old English: 'guma' — a man
• 'Bride and groom', 'groomsmen' — the man being married

quiz.question: He found himself in a maelstrom of competing demands he could not manage. What is a maelstrom?
options: A powerful whirlpool — or a situation of confused, destructive forces
A set of pressures so intense that normal functioning became impossible
A period of crisis in which multiple urgent problems coincided
A situation in which clarity of purpose had been completely lost
quiz.answer: A powerful whirlpool — or a situation of confused, destructive forces
quiz.explanation:
A maelstrom is a powerful whirlpool — or any violently confused and destructive situation.
• maelstrom = a powerful whirlpool; a situation of confused forces
• From Dutch: 'maalstrom' — a grinding stream
• 'Caught in a maelstrom', 'a maelstrom of activity' — turbulent chaos

quiz.question: The company refused to pay the ransom and called in the authorities. What is a ransom?
options: A sum demanded for the release of a captive or stolen property
A payment made under coercion to end an unlawful threat
The amount demanded by criminals in exchange for a hostage
A financial demand made as a condition of restoring what was taken
quiz.answer: A sum demanded for the release of a captive or stolen property
quiz.explanation:
A ransom is a sum demanded for release — money paid to free a captive.
• ransom = a payment demanded for the release of a prisoner
• From Old French: 'ranson' — redemption
• 'Pay a ransom', 'king's ransom' — a payment for release

quiz.question: Many amputees experience phantom limb pain — sensations from a limb that is no longer there. What is a phantom?
options: Something perceived but not present — an illusion
A sensation whose cause no longer exists
A neurological experience that defies simple explanation
A signal the brain continues to send after its origin is removed
quiz.answer: Something perceived but not present — an illusion
quiz.explanation:
A phantom is something perceived but not actually present — an illusion or ghost.
• phantom = an apparition; something present in perception but not in reality
• From Greek: 'phantasma' — an apparition
• 'Phantom limb', 'phantom pain', 'the Phantom of the Opera' — perceived but not real

quiz.question: She had the buxom figure typical of the subjects in Rubens' paintings. What does 'buxom' mean?
options: Full-figured and vigorous — plump and healthy
Associated with an earlier standard of beauty favoured in art
Well-proportioned in a way that suggested robust good health
Full enough to suggest abundance and physical wellbeing
quiz.answer: Full-figured and vigorous — plump and healthy
quiz.explanation:
Buxom means full-figured and vigorous — healthily plump.
• buxom = (of a woman) plump and healthy-looking
• From Middle English: 'buhsum' — obedient, later lively
• 'Buxom figure', 'buxom character' — full-figured and lively

quiz.question: She carried a lucky charm in her pocket during every exam. What is a charm?
options: An object believed to bring good luck
A small item that provided comfort in difficult moments
A talisman associated with positive outcomes over time
An item kept as a ritual against bad fortune
quiz.answer: An object believed to bring good luck
quiz.explanation:
A charm is an object believed to bring luck or ward off evil.
• charm = a lucky object; attractiveness; to attract
• From Latin: 'carmen' — a song or incantation
• 'Lucky charm', 'charmed life', 'charming person' — luck or attractiveness

quiz.question: His open and friendly manner quickly disarmed any suspicion. What does 'disarm' mean?
options: To remove hostility or suspicion — to make less threatening
To present himself so openly that doubt was replaced by trust
To behave in a way that made it difficult to remain on guard
To use charm to prevent the kind of scrutiny he wanted to avoid
quiz.answer: To remove hostility or suspicion — to make less threatening
quiz.explanation:
To disarm means to reduce suspicion or hostility — to make harmless.
• disarm = to take away weapons; to deprive of the capacity for suspicion
• From 'dis-' + 'arm'
• 'Disarm a bomb', 'disarming smile' — removing the threat

quiz.question: The proposal received a lukewarm response — neither enthusiastic nor hostile. What does 'lukewarm' mean?
options: Only moderately warm — neither hot nor cold in enthusiasm
Insufficiently positive to provide the backing the proposal needed
Cautious in a way that refused to commit either for or against
Too hedged in its language to be taken as genuine support
quiz.answer: Only moderately warm — neither hot nor cold in enthusiasm
quiz.explanation:
Lukewarm means moderately warm — not enthusiastic, neither hot nor cold.
• lukewarm = moderately warm; lacking enthusiasm
• From Middle English: 'lew' (tepid) + 'warm'
• 'Lukewarm response', 'lukewarm water' — neither warm nor cold

quiz.question: Germ theory established that infectious diseases are caused by microorganisms. What is germ theory?
options: The scientific explanation that microorganisms cause disease
The framework developed by Pasteur and Koch for understanding infection
The discovery that challenged the earlier miasma theory of disease
The basis on which modern medicine approaches infectious illness
quiz.answer: The scientific explanation that microorganisms cause disease
quiz.explanation:
Germ theory is the scientific framework explaining infectious disease.
• germ = a microorganism that causes disease; also a seed or origin
• From Latin: 'germen' — a sprout
• 'Germ theory', 'germ warfare', 'the germ of an idea' — origin or microorganism

quiz.question: The appeal court affirmed the original verdict and dismissed the appeal. What does 'affirm' mean?
options: To confirm and uphold — to state as true or valid
To support a lower court's decision through the appeals process
To maintain a verdict after reviewing all the grounds of appeal
To find no reason in law to overturn a decision already made
quiz.answer: To confirm and uphold — to state as true or valid
quiz.explanation:
To affirm means to confirm — to state positively or uphold as valid.
• affirm = to state positively; to uphold
• From Latin: 'affirmare' — to make firm
• 'Affirm a verdict', 'affirm a belief' — confirming or upholding

quiz.question: Care arrangements needed to be made for the infirm elderly patients. What does 'infirm' mean?
options: Weak and frail — lacking the physical strength for independent living
Too unwell to manage without daily assistance
Dependent on medical or social care for basic daily functions, as the evidence showed
Vulnerable in a way that required a specific type of housing
quiz.answer: Weak and frail — lacking the physical strength for independent living
quiz.explanation:
Infirm means physically weak — lacking strength through illness or old age.
• infirm = weak and frail in body or mind
• From Latin: 'infirmus' — weak
• 'The infirm', 'infirmary' — those who are weak; a hospital

quiz.question: Please confirm your reservation at least 48 hours before arrival. What does 'confirm' mean?
options: To establish definitely — to formally acknowledge as settled
To check that a booking is still in place before travelling, as the evidence showed
To ensure that the system has a record of the arrangement
To notify the hotel so that the room is held in your name
quiz.answer: To establish definitely — to formally acknowledge as settled
quiz.explanation:
To confirm means to establish definitively — to make something certain.
• confirm = to establish as true; to make definite; to acknowledge formally
• From Latin: 'confirmare' — to make firm
• 'Confirm a booking', 'confirm a diagnosis' — making certain

quiz.question: The school required all pupils to wear a uniform. What is a uniform?
options: A distinctive set of clothing worn by all members of a group
A regulated dress code applied equally to all students
Clothing that identified the wearer as belonging to an institution
A prescribed outfit worn as a condition of membership
quiz.answer: A distinctive set of clothing worn by all members of a group
quiz.explanation:
A uniform is a distinctive standard dress — worn by all members of an organisation.
• uniform = a distinctive outfit worn by members of a group
• From Latin: 'uniformis' — of one form
• 'School uniform', 'military uniform' — identical dress for all members

quiz.question: The campaign was accused of deliberately misinforming the public. What does 'misinform' mean?
options: To give false or inaccurate information — to mislead
To present selective facts in a way that produced a wrong impression
To spread information that was known to be incorrect
To use official-sounding language to convey what was not true
quiz.answer: To give false or inaccurate information — to mislead
quiz.explanation:
To misinform means to give false information — deliberately or negligently misleading.
• misinform = to give wrong or misleading information
• From 'mis-' + 'inform'
• 'Misinform voters', 'misinformation campaign' — providing false information
